item.title: movies
item.description: a small list of movies that have shaped my love for cinema, not that i even go to the cinema if you’re a normalfag, you may find most movies of the movies here lean toward the obscure side and you probably wouldnt like most of them psychotraumatic movies that could fuck you up emotionally, be it by watching a mental illness take its toll on a character or by seeing them go through traumatic events these movies are very extreme, as there is alot of sadism, perversion and inhumanity (not all of them are this extreme though, e.

item.title: lisp
item.description: a programming language some resources: why lisp is superior http://paulgraham.com/icad.html great video on lisp debugging in emacs https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jBBS4FeY7XM https://www.cliki.net/naming+conventions

item.title: emacs
item.description: 1 useful describe-char 2 query syntax of a char (char-syntax (string-to-char "-")) 3 ob-async with latex this seems to be the only way to use ob-async with latex blocks: #+HEADER: :fit yes :imagemagick yes #+HEADER: :packages '("\\usepackage{tikz}") #+BEGIN_SRC latex :file /tmp/myimg.png :results file graphics :async \tikz\draw (0,0) circle (10px); \tikz\draw[fill=gray!30!white] (0,0) ellipse (28pt and 20pt); \tikz\draw[line width=2mm] (0,0) arc (30:200:1cm); \tikz\fill[color=red] (0,0) rectangle (2,1); #+END_SRC 4 reset variable to its default value (setq foo (eval (car (get 'foo 'standard-value)))) 5 org mode 5.

item.title: maxima
item.description: a computer algebra system written in lisp

item.title: maxima in lisp
item.description: i ended up giving up on using maxima as a visualization/symbolic math computation library for lisp, it wasnt made with this usecase in mind i guess, and so its an utter nightmare to lisp with it maxima is written in lisp and so it can be used as a modified/extended lisp runtime 1 how to to start the lisp runtime use the command: rmaxima -r 'to_lisp();' can also run this in emacs’ slime/sly with (sly "rmaxima -r to_lisp();") alternatively, we can clone maxima’s source code to the subdirectory local-projects of the quicklisp installation directory (usually ~/quicklisp unless you’ve modified it), follow the instructions at https://sourceforge.
